text: The surgeon positions the terminal ileum adjacent to the transverse colon and places stay sutures to secure the anastomosis. The first stay suture is cut to twenty centimeters for use in both stitches, with the second suture placed more distally for enhanced control of the anastomosis.

The surgeon opens the anti-mesenteric border of the small bowel and the large bowel, creating an opening on the specimen side. The large bowel is positioned more medially toward the mesenteric side to facilitate stapler navigation through the right lower quadrant port. Cautery and cutting are used to achieve bowel entry, with the mesentery mobilized in preparation for stapler insertion.

The surgeon rotates the stapler so that the green side is positioned on the colonic side, allowing it to easily enter the channel. Both stapler loads are successfully navigated into the lumens for the anastomosis.

The surgeon elevates the anastomosis anteriorly to allow the mesentery to fall away, holding up both stay sutures. The stapler is opened to let extraneous tissue fall away, then tucked in before closing the stapler, achieving excellent approximation and closure of the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses the FireFly imaging system to confirm perfusion of the small bowel and colon before firing the stapler, ensuring no extraneous tissue is caught between the stapler jaws.

The surgeon skeletonizes the mesentery on the small bowel side, ensuring it falls away perpendicularly to facilitate stapler navigation. The small bowel and colon are positioned perpendicular to the stapler, with the stapler tip placed distal to the proximal stay suture to ensure adequate perfusion. Additional arms are used to manipulate and hold the bowel for easier stapler navigation.

The surgeon applies a second stapler perpendicular to the original staple line between the small and large bowel, aiming for a clean, common staple line. The video shows tissue incorporation, luminal placement of the stapler, and the colon rolling away, with emphasis on ensuring adequate mesentery dissection up to the proximal suture.
